series.name: The Eighth Layer AI Field Guide

campaigns.title: MCP Explained Without the Hype
campaigns.hook: MCP is how AI stops guessing and starts using tools.
campaigns.guide_promise: A plain-English guide to Model Context Protocol, why it matters, and how builders should think about tool-connected AI.
campaigns.angle: Explain MCP as a practical bridge between models, tools, data, and workflows.
slides: AI needs tools, not just text.
Models guess when context is missing.
Tools turn AI into a workflow surface.
It gives AI a standard way to connect.

guide.updated_label: Updated for the current MCP ecosystem
guide.hero_promise: A builder's field guide to MCP, what it connects, when to use it, and how to start without overbuilding.

summary_cards.label: The plain-English version
summary_cards.text: MCP gives AI apps a standard way to reach tools, files, data, and workflows through controlled servers.

summary_cards.label: The builder move
summary_cards.text: Do not start by connecting everything. Start with one repeated workflow and one safe tool.

summary_cards.label: The risk to manage
summary_cards.text: Tool access turns AI from drafting partner into operator. Permissions, review, and logs matter.

sections.heading: The model asks. The client brokers. The server exposes capabilities.
sections.body: MCP is an open protocol for connecting AI hosts to servers that expose capabilities. The point is not magic. The point is a shared contract, so builders do not need custom glue code for every app, tool, and data source.
bullets: Host: the AI app or agent experience where the user works.
Client: the protocol-speaking connector inside that host.
Server: the service that exposes capabilities over MCP.
Capability: a tool, resource, or prompt the assistant can discover and use.

sections.heading: Tools, resources, and prompts are the core pieces.
sections.body: A useful MCP setup is not magic. It is a small menu of capabilities the assistant can call when the workflow needs them.

cards.label: Tool
cards.text: An action with defined inputs and outputs, like search issues, query a database, create a branch, or open a browser page.

cards.label: Resource
cards.text: Readable context, like a file, schema, document, ticket, or knowledge base entry.

cards.label: Prompt
cards.text: A reusable instruction pattern, like summarize this repo, draft a release note, or analyze this issue queue.

bullets: If the model needs to do something, think tool.
If the model needs to read something, think resource.
If the team needs a repeatable instruction pattern, think prompt.

sections.heading: Use MCP when the workflow repeats.
sections.body: If you only need one answer, chat may be enough. If the assistant repeatedly needs the same tool, file, API, or system of record, MCP starts to make sense.
bullets: Good fit: repeated work with clear inputs and outputs.
Good fit: read-only access to context the assistant needs often.
Good fit: team workflows where the same tool connection can be reused.
Bad fit: one-off tasks, unclear data boundaries, or sensitive writes without approval.

sections.heading: Start with one safe integration.
sections.body: The first win is not a giant agent stack. The first win is a narrow capability that removes repeated copy-paste and still gives humans control.
checklist: Name one workflow you repeat every week.
Write the outcome in one sentence.
List the minimum context the assistant needs.
Define the exact jobs the server should support.
Start with the minimum set of tools and resources.
Write tight input schemas and predictable outputs.
Decide transport and authentication before rollout.
Add a human review point before any write action.
Log what the tool did and what it touched.
Test with an inspector or low-risk workflow before broader use.

sections.heading: MCP makes permissions a product decision.
sections.body: Once an assistant can use tools, security stops being an afterthought. The safest MCP workflow is scoped, inspectable, and easy to turn off.
bullets: Use authentication for every sensitive endpoint.
Authorize per tool and per resource, not only per server.
Use least privilege credentials.
Add explicit allowlists for dangerous actions.
Keep secrets out of prompts and generated content.
Prefer read-only until the workflow proves value.
Require approval before writes, deletes, payments, or messages.
Use rate limits, timeouts, and clear failure behavior.
Keep logs so a human can understand what happened.

guide.starter_prompt: I want to design one safe MCP workflow. Ask me for the workflow, the tool or data source, the minimum context needed, the action the assistant should take, the risk level, the approval point, and the log I should keep. Then give me a small first version that starts read-only.
glossary: Model Context Protocol, a standard for connecting AI apps to external capabilities.
The AI app or agent environment where the user works.
The protocol layer that connects the host to MCP servers.
The service that exposes tools, resources, or prompts.
A callable action the assistant can use.
How the client and server communicate, often local stdio or remote HTTP.
The boundary around what the assistant can access or do.

repo_reading_list.why: Reference servers show the core patterns and also warn that examples are not production-ready by default.
The TypeScript SDK is useful for understanding the server and client primitives in a common web stack.
The Python SDK is useful for data, automation, and scripting workflows.
GitHub's server shows how issues, pull requests, files, and reviews become an AI-accessible workflow surface.
Inspector-style tooling reminds builders to test the server before putting it inside real work.

source_notes.name: Model Context Protocol Docs
source_notes.note: Use the official docs for the core mental model, host, client, server, tools, resources, and prompts.

source_notes.name: MCP Example Servers
source_notes.note: Use examples to see how different servers expose real capabilities, then decide what belongs in your own workflow.

source_notes.name: modelcontextprotocol/servers
source_notes.note: Reference servers are useful for learning patterns, but the repo warns that examples are not production-ready by default.

source_notes.name: GitHub MCP Server
source_notes.note: A practical example of turning issues, pull requests, files, and reviews into an AI-accessible workflow surface.

campaigns.title: 5 AI Skills That Make You More Than a Prompt User

slides: Chat is not always the answer.

campaigns.title: The AI Tool Stack for Builders Who Actually Ship
campaigns.hook: Your AI stack is your new operating system.

slides: AI gets better when the workflow gets clearer.
Do: give the job and outcome.

campaigns.hook: Better prompts start before you type.

campaigns.title: AI Fluency: Consumer, User, Builder, Operator
campaigns.hook: Most people stop at using AI. Builders learn to operate it.

slides: Where are you on the ladder?
